perturbation.original_text: (o) "Company Field" means the design, development, manufacture, marketing, promotion, advertising, sourcing, distribution and sale of solid hardwood and engineered wood flooring products by or for any Company Entity.
perturbation.changed_text: (o) "Company Field" means the design, development, manufacture, marketing, promotion, advertising, sourcing, distribution and sale of any flooring products by or for any Company Entity, **subject to Arizona's prior written approval of all marketing materials. Such approval shall not be unreasonably withheld.**
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of 'Company Field' to include any flooring products, but adds a requirement for Arizona's approval of marketing materials. This creates an in-text contradiction because elsewhere, the agreement grants the Company Entities certain rights within the 'Company Field' without any such approval requirement. Specifically, the Arizona Know-How Grant in section 4.1 grants the Company a perpetual, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Know-How for use in the Company Field throughout the world, which would now be subjected to Arizona's approval creating an inconsistency.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Arizona Know-How Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a perpetual, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Know-How for use in the Company Field throughout the world.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined the 'Company Field' as applying to specific flooring products (hardwood and engineered wood). The changed text broadens this definition to 'any flooring products' but introduces a requirement for Arizona's approval of marketing materials within this field. This directly contradicts section 4.1, the 'Arizona Know-How Grant,' which grants the Company a perpetual, non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use the Arizona Licensed Know-How within the 'Company Field' without any mention of needing Arizona's marketing approval. By adding this approval requirement in the definition of 'Company Field,' the change effectively places a restriction on the license granted in section 4.1, creating a strong in-text contradiction and potential unenforceability issues, thus fitting the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Arizona Licensed Trademark Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world **in a way that will not be damaging to the name and goodwill of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks**, only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation introduces a subjective limitation on the use of Arizona Licensed Trademarks: 'in a way that will not be damaging to the name and goodwill of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.' This directly contradicts the objective standards for quality control outlined in Section 6.3, which specifies concrete requirements (quality of goods/services, not tarnishing reputation, compliance with laws, trademark notices). This contradiction creates uncertainty because it is unclear which standard applies and how a 'damaging' use would be determined. Section 6.3(a) states that The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.3 Quality Control. The Buyer Entities acknowledge the importance of Arizona's exercise of quality control over the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks to preserve the continued integrity and validity of the Seller Licensed Trademarks and to protect the value and goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks, and accordingly: (a) The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks. (b) The Company shall not tarnish or bring into disrepute the reputation of or goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks or Arizona. (c) The Company shall use the Seller Licensed Trademarks at all times in compliance with all applicable Laws. (d) The Company shall include trademark and other notices in connection with the use of the Seller Licensed Trademarks as reasonably requested by Arizona from time to time. (e) The Company shall upon Arizona's reasonable request from time to time, supply to Arizona representative samples and/or written descriptions, as appropriate, of uses made by the Company of the Seller Licensed Trademarks. (f) The Buyer Entities acknowledge that this license grant does not include, and the Company shall receive no rights under this Agreement or the Stock Purchase Agreement, to use any Trademark that is confusingly similar to or derivative of a Seller Licensed Trademark (other than the Seller Licensed Trademarks themselves as expressly authorized hereunder).
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a subjective standard ('in a way that will not be damaging to the name and goodwill of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks') for trademark use in Section 6.1. This directly contradicts the objective quality control standards outlined in Section 6.3(a), which specifies concrete requirements such as the quality of goods/services, not tarnishing reputation, and compliance with laws. This creates a direct conflict and ambiguity because it is unclear which standard applies and how 'damaging' use would be determined.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1 Term and Termination. (a) Unless earlier terminated pursuant to the provisions hereof, the term of this Agreement and the licenses and other grants of rights (and related obligations) under this Agreement shall (i) with respect to the Arizona Licensed Trademarks, be for the Arizona Trademark License Term, (ii) with respect to the Diamond Licensed Trademarks, be for the Diamond Trademark License Term, (iii) with respect to the Phase- Out Marks, be for the term set forth in Section 6.6, and (iv) with respect to Copyrights, Know-How and Patents, be in perpetuity.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 Term and Termination. (a) Unless earlier terminated pursuant to the provisions hereof, the term of this Agreement and the licenses and other grants of rights (and related obligations) under this Agreement shall (i) with respect to the Arizona Licensed Trademarks, be for the Arizona Trademark License Term, **unless Arizona ceases to use the ARMSTRONG trademark, in which case the license terminates immediately**, (ii) with respect to the Diamond Licensed Trademarks, be for the Diamond Trademark License Term, (iii) with respect to the Phase- Out Marks, be for the term set forth in Section 6.6, and (iv) with respect to Copyrights, Know-How and Patents, be in perpetuity.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text adds a condition for the early termination of the Arizona Licensed Trademark license: if Arizona ceases to use the ARMSTRONG trademark. This introduces an in-text contradiction with Section 12.1(b), which outlines the general conditions for termination of the entire agreement or parts thereof due to material breach. Section 12.1(b) states that Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within thirty (30) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.1 Term and Termination. (b) Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within thirty (30) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Arizona Licensed Trademark license lasts for the Arizona Trademark License Term. The changed text adds a condition that terminates the license immediately if Arizona ceases to use the ARMSTRONG trademark. This directly contradicts section 12.1(b), which outlines the process for terminating the agreement (or parts of it) due to a material breach, requiring a 30-day notice and opportunity to remedy. The changed text allows for immediate termination under a different condition, creating a conflict in how termination is handled, leading to a strong in-text contradiction.
